theme: Close Every Loop — Every Prop, Every State, Every Interaction Should Speak

ideas.title: Error Count Stat Card — Red Flag in the Stats Bar
ideas.description: The StatsBar shows Total / Active / Idle / Sync / Vibe — but Errors, the most operationally urgent signal in the entire dashboard, have no dedicated card. The only way to see error count right now is via the tab badge (invisible if you're already on dashboard) or by visually scanning all worker cards. Fix this with a 6th stat card: `<div className='stat-card error-count'>`. Compute `const errorCount = workers.filter(w => w.status === 'error').length` inside StatsBar (already available). Render only when `errorCount > 0`, with a CSS `animation:stat-error-in 0.3s cubic-bezier(0.34,1.56,0.64,1)` entrance. Style: `border-color:rgba(239,68,68,0.45); background:rgba(239,68,68,0.06)`. Value in bold red. Label: `❌ Errors`. When it drops back to 0, fade out: add `stat-card--exiting` class for 300ms then remove. App.css: `.stat-card.error-count { --stat-accent:#ef4444; } .stat-card.error-count .stat-value { color:var(--red); font-weight:900; }` `@keyframes stat-error-in { from{transform:scale(0.7) translateY(10px);opacity:0} to{transform:none;opacity:1} }`. WHY: Error count is a triage signal — it should be the most visible number on screen when nonzero. Burying it means engineers miss incidents. One stat card at the top of the dashboard changes the dashboard from 'pretty monitor' to 'actual ops tool'.

ideas.title: Manual Sync + R Keyboard Shortcut — Respect the Power User
ideas.description: There is no way to force an immediate data refresh. The only Retry buttons appear on error banners (rare). Power users — engineers babysitting deploys — will stare at stale data for 30 seconds and not know why. Two-part fix: (1) In the stats-bar area, add a small `<button className='sync-now-btn' onClick={syncFromGitHub} disabled={isSyncing} aria-label='Sync now'>↺</button>` that spins when `isSyncing`. CSS: `.sync-now-btn { background:none; border:1px solid var(--border); border-radius:6px; padding:3px 8px; font-size:0.75rem; color:var(--text-muted); cursor:pointer; transition:color 0.15s,border-color 0.15s; } .sync-now-btn:hover { color:var(--text); border-color:var(--accent); } .sync-now-btn[disabled] { animation:spin 0.7s linear infinite; }` `@keyframes spin{to{transform:rotate(360deg)}}`. (2) In the existing keyboard shortcut useEffect in App.jsx, add to the handler: `else if ((e.key === 'r' || e.key === 'R') && !isSyncing) syncFromGitHub()`. R for refresh is muscle memory from every monitoring tool ever made. WHY: A dashboard you trust is a dashboard you keep open. Trust comes from knowing you can poke it when you need to. The R shortcut costs 2 lines. The sync button costs 5 lines of CSS. Together they signal 'this tool was built by people who use it.'

ideas.title: Vibe Change Cinematic Announcement — Your New Vibe Has Arrived
ideas.description:
CARRY-FORWARD from review #11. The current vibe change experience: confetti fires if vibe improves (great), nothing happens if it worsens (tragic). Both directions deserve ceremony. Add a 2.5-second full-overlay vibe change announcement card. In App.jsx add `const [vibeAnnouncement, setVibeAnnouncement] = useState(null)`. In the vibe streak useEffect, when `prevKey !== null && prevKey !== teamVibe.key`, call `setVibeAnnouncement({ from: prevKey, to: teamVibe.key, label: teamVibe.label, emoji: teamVibe.emoji })`. Then `setTimeout(() => setVibeAnnouncement(null), 2500)`. Render as overlay: `{vibeAnnouncement && (
Vibe shift
{vibeAnnouncement.emoji}
{vibeAnnouncement.label}
)}`. CSS: `.vibe-announce{position:fixed;inset:0;z-index:1000;display:flex;align-items:center;justify-content:center;pointer-events:none}` `.vibe-announce__card{background:var(--surface);border:1px solid var(--border);border-radius:20px;padding:2rem 3rem;text-align:center;box-shadow:0 24px 80px rgba(0,0,0,0.6);animation:vibe-announce-in 0.5s cubic-bezier(0.34,1.56,0.64,1) both,vibe-announce-out 0.4s ease 2s forwards}` `@keyframes vibe-announce-in{from{opacity:0;transform:scale(0.7) translateY(30px)}to{opacity:1;transform:none}}` `@keyframes vibe-announce-out{to{opacity:0;transform:scale(0.9) translateY(-20px)}}`. Suppress during initial load (prevKey !== null guard). WHY: Vibe is the heartbeat of this product. When it shifts, it should feel like a moment — not a silent CSS class swap.

ideas.title: Desk Monitor SOS Morse Code on Error — The Desk Is Screaming

CARRY-FORWARD from review #11. When a worker hits error status, the desk gets a red tint — but the `.desk__monitor` does nothing special. Error desks deserve a visceral visual signal: SOS in morse code flashing on the monitor. In Office.css, add `.desk--error .desk__monitor { animation: sos-morse 4.2s step-start infinite; box-shadow: 0 0 0 2px var(--red), 0 0 12px rgba(239,68,68,0.4); }` and define the animation with SOS morse timing (·  ·  · — :

— ·  ·  ·): `@keyframes sos-morse { 0%{background:var(--red);opacity:1} 5%{opacity:0} 10%{opacity:1} 15%{opacity:0} 20%{opacity:1} 25%{opacity:0} 33%{background:#ff6600;opacity:1} 40%{opacity:0} 49%{opacity:1} 56%{opacity:0} 65%{opacity:1} 70%{opacity:0} 75%{background:var(--red);opacity:1} 80%{opacity:0} 83%{opacity:1} 88%{opacity:0} 93%{opacity:1} 98%{opacity:0} 100%{opacity:1} }`. Zero JS, CSS-only. Also add it to the reduced-motion override block as `animation:none`. WHY: SOS is a universal distress signal. Engineers recognise the pattern subconsciously. A morse-code monitor creates ambient urgency without banners, alerts, or noise — just light.
Bloberto's Completion Corkboard — The Wins Wall
ideas.description: CARRY-FORWARD from review #11. The dead wall space between the whiteboard and the manager desk is the perfect location for a tiny corkboard celebrating completions in real-time. Add `.office-corkboard` div in Office.jsx (left:21%, top:3%), 72px wide × 48px tall. Background: brown border + `background-image:repeating-linear-gradient(45deg,rgba(0,0,0,0.06) 0,rgba(0,0,0,0.06) 1px,transparent 0,transparent 50%) center/8px 8px`. Pass `recentCompletions` prop from App.jsx: `activityLog.filter(e=>e.type==='complete').slice(-3)`. Render up to 3 `.corkboard-note` children — one per completion — with pastel backgrounds (yellow/green/pink), rotated slightly via inline `--note-rot`, showing worker initial + task truncated to 12 chars. On note mount: `animation:note-pin 0.3s cubic-bezier(0.34,1.56,0.64,1)`. WHY: The office floor only reacts to failure (error glitches) and vibe. It never celebrates success. Three sticky notes that appear as the team ships is the difference between a monitoring dashboard and a team culture artifact.

ideas.title: Bloberto Stress Quotes on Active Errors — The Manager Notices
ideas.description: The speech bubble system uses VIBE_QUOTES with 4-5 lines per vibe state — but errors are invisible to Bloberto. When `errorCount > 0`, the manager should betray some awareness, even via corporate doublespeak. Add an `ERROR_QUOTES` array to Office.jsx: `['I see the red. I see it.', 'We are treating this as a learning opportunity. Live.', 'I am choosing not to look at the monitor right now.', 'Everything is fine. I have defined \'fine\' very broadly.', 'Have you tried turning the engineer off and on again?', 'The SLA is... flexible. Very flexible.']`. In the Character component, accept an `errorCount` prop. In the ambient interval effect, check: `if (errorCount > 0 && Math.random() < 0.6) quotes = ERROR_QUOTES` — giving a 60% chance that an ambient bubble during an error event is stress-flavored. In Office.jsx render, pass `errorCount={nonMgr.filter(w=>w.status==='error').length}` to the Bloberto Character. WHY: Right now Bloberto blissfully talks about 'alignment' and 'shipping it' while the floor is literally glitching red. A manager who notices errors — even nervously, even corpspeak — closes the character loop and makes the product feel like a world with cause and effect.

ideas.title: Idle Worker Thought Bubbles — The Floor Thinks
ideas.description: Idle workers wander the floor but say absolutely nothing. Working workers have typing dots. Ghosts have OOO bubbles. But idle is a void. Add random thought bubbles to idle wandering characters, firing once every 45–90s per worker. In the Character component, for `variant === 'idle'` add an interval: `useEffect(() => { if (variant !== 'idle') return; const id = setInterval(() => { setThoughtBubble(IDLE_THOUGHTS[Math.floor(Math.random()*IDLE_THOUGHTS.length)]); setTimeout(()=>setThoughtBubble(null), 3500); }, 45000 + Math.random()*45000); return ()=>clearInterval(id); }, [variant])`. Define `IDLE_THOUGHTS` in Office.jsx: `['Should\'ve pushed before lunch...', 'Is this a good time to refactor the refactor?', 'Coffee #3 imminent.', 'I\'m not blocking anything. Technically.', 'Just gonna check Twitter for a sec.', 'Maybe if I stare at the backlog it\'ll shrink.', 'PR approved. I\'ll merge it... later.']`. Render as `.thought-bubble` (round cloud style, different from speech-bubble triangle): `position:absolute; bottom:calc(100%+6px); background:rgba(20,18,50,0.88); border-radius:12px; padding:5px 9px; font-size:0.62rem; color:#c4b5fd; white-space:nowrap; pointer-events:none; z-index:150`. WHY: Idle doesn't mean absent. Thought bubbles make the floor feel inhabited during slow periods — and give idle workers the personality they currently lack. This is the feature that makes people take screenshots and share them in Slack.

ideas.title: Animated Door on Worker Entry — Someone Just Walked In
ideas.description: When a new worker arrives, their Character plays the `enter-office` animation (slide up from bottom). But the door below just sits there. Spatially, the character came from nowhere. Close this gap: track `doorOpenActive` state in Office.jsx. When `workingWorkers.length` increases (compare to a ref), set `doorOpenActive(true)` and clear it after 800ms. Add `door--open` class to `.office-door`: `.office-door--open .office-door__icon { animation:door-swing 0.7s cubic-bezier(0.34,1.56,0.64,1) both; }` `@keyframes door-swing { 0%{transform:scale(1) rotate(0)} 35%{transform:scale(1.35) rotate(-20deg)} 70%{transform:scale(1.1) rotate(5deg)} 100%{transform:scale(1) rotate(0)} }`. Also add a brief warm glow to the door frame: `@keyframes door-glow{0%,100%{filter:none}35%{filter:drop-shadow(0 0 6px rgba(255,210,100,0.6))}}`. Track previous working count via a `useRef`. WHY: The door is a stage prop waiting to become a story beat. When Carlos walks in, the door should react. A 700ms door swing is the spatial punctuation that says: someone just arrived. The enter-office animation becomes 3× more meaningful when the door it came from swings open.

ideas.title: Coffee Corner Vibe Reaction — The Machine Knows
ideas.description: The coffee corner renders the same ☕ emoji at the same size with the same label regardless of whether the team is crushing it or half-asleep. It is the only piece of office furniture that does not react to vibe. Fix this with CSS-only, data-vibe-driven styles. In Office.jsx, add `data-vibe={vibe}` to the `.coffee-corner` div. CSS: `.coffee-corner[data-vibe='crushing'] .coffee-corner__emoji { animation:coffee-surge 0.7s ease-in-out infinite; }` `@keyframes coffee-surge{0%,100%{transform:scale(1) rotate(-3deg)}50%{transform:scale(1.2) rotate(3deg)}}` `.coffee-corner[data-vibe='slow-day'] .coffee-corner__emoji { opacity:0.55; filter:grayscale(0.4); }` `.coffee-corner[data-vibe='after-hours'] .coffee-corner__label::after { content:' (decaf)'; font-size:0.38rem; color:var(--text-muted); }`. Also add a CSS steam element above the cup: `.coffee-corner__steam { position:absolute; top:-10px; left:50%; transform:translateX(-50%); font-size:0.55rem; color:rgba(255,255,255,0.35); animation:steam-rise 2s ease-in infinite; pointer-events:none; content:'~'; }` with faster animation during crushing, none during slow-day. WHY: The coffee corner is a prop that could be a character. Vibe-reactive coffee is environment storytelling that rewards attentive viewers — the kind of hidden detail that becomes a feature people mention when they recommend this dashboard to a colleague.

ideas.title: Confetti Rain on Vibe Drop — The Other Direction Also Deserves Ceremony
ideas.description:
Confetti fires upward on vibe improvement — which is lovely. But a vibe drop (e.g. crushing → on-fire, in-flow → slow-day) produces exactly zero visual response. The asymmetry is narratively weird: good news gets a party, bad news gets silence. Add a 'confetti rain' effect for vibe drops — same layer, same pieces, but inverted direction. In the vibe streak useEffect in App.jsx, after the confetti burst condition, add: `else if (prevKey !== null && vibeRanking[teamVibe.key] < (vibeRanking[prevKey] ?? 999)) { setConfettiRain(true); setTimeout(() => setConfettiRain(false), 2000); }`. Add `[confettiRain, setConfettiRain] = useState(false)`. Render alongside the existing confetti layer: `{confettiRain && (
{Array.from({length:20},(_,i)=>)}
)}`. CSS: `.confetti-layer--rain .confetti-piece { animation:confetti-fall var(--dur,1.8s) ease-in both; animation-delay:calc(var(--i)*0.07s); }` `@keyframes confetti-fall { from{transform:translateY(-40px) rotate(0deg);opacity:1} to{transform:translateY(calc(100vh + 20px)) rotate(720deg);opacity:0} }`. Use darker, more muted piece colors for rain vs. the bright celebration palette. WHY: Every good narrative has emotional range. Right now the app celebrates but never mourns. Rain confetti is darkly funny — a visual joke about the team's fortunes reversing. It will make engineers laugh in the same moment it confirms bad news, which is exactly the tone this product lives in.
